inline_comments.message:
This is very fancy feature , I am supportive for this feature as long as it can co-exist/co-work with existing implementing practices .
So my questions: 1, is this a tool to generate python source codes so that developers could modify/customize them?
2, is that possible to add other NBI/SBI translation codes and exposing them without putting them into the YAML?
thanks

For the questions:

1. It is not a tool to generate python code. It is  a set of python code that read YAML file and act according to it.
2. This spec will only consider definition in YAML files.

It is a very good start of this work. Thank you for moving it forward. Just a few observations and comments:

1. Regarding "act according to it", which actions do you have in mind after the code reads YAML?

2. Can you expand the full definition of YAML file as you mentioned above? I admit that myself, as a normal person, won't be able to write an YAML yet after reading this document in its current format.

3. The same type of work (or a prior art) has already been done in OpenStack Gluon project. There is a well defined and very mature specification in https://github.com/openstack/gluon/blob/master/doc/source/devref/gluon_api_spec.inc.

I might be wrong, but I wouldn't expect the APIs are explicitly defined in YAML.

Please refer to:

(1) https://wiki.onap.org/display/DW/RESTful+API+Design+Specification provides a good guidance of how the resources would look like, and the APIs are those CRUD actions over those resources.

(2) The same type of work (or a prior art) has already been done in OpenStack Gluon project. There is a well defined and very mature specification in https://github.com/openstack/gluon/blob/master/doc/source/devref/gluon_api_spec.inc.

I think we are mixing HTTP methods with YAML resources here. Please refer to https://wiki.onap.org/display/DW/RESTful+API+Design+Specification for a good explanation of resources and RESTful APIs using HTTP.
Can you give an explicit example of those "`parameters` are the variables in the URL"?

Those are standard methods of using HTTP-based RESTful APIs to handle those resources. It is redundant here because it is off the focus of this document as mentioned at the beginning and clarified in comments: "This spec will only consider definition in YAML files."
Those are actually examples. Can we have a full, normative definition?

Certainly, the full, normative and machine-parsable definition of YAML is missing. Without this full, normative definition, how can a parser parse, validate and take actions accordingly?

I guess we don't want to hardcode any tool, do we?
Shouldn't Plugins call southbound VIM APIs?
Shouldn't YAML Parser validate the conformance?

(1) I don't see a conflict between the guide and this spec. The guide said that we should not use url definition like this:

 /api/petstore/v1/pets/getalldogs

 /api/petstore/v1/pets/createdog

 /api/petstore/v1/pets/deletedog

In the spec, I just define supported http methods, the url will not change. And I am using the swagger rule here. So that it will be easier to covert to swagger file latter.


(2) I have checked the gluon spec, there are many good point to take reference.
see above answer
in this example, it will be vimid, tenantid and hostid. Application can use these parameters to get extra information.
But we are not support all the http method for all url, right? In the example, only 'get' is supported.
actually, the 'host' in this spec is a normative definition. It might be extended in future, but for now, it is full( unless you tell me something is missing :) )

There is no intention to hardcode anything. Could you elaborate what is "full normative definition". I believe the example in this spec is enough for api "/hosts". I don't plan to add all the content of YAML in the spec.

Yes, this workitem is to use a restclient in plugin to call VIM api. because we are using openstack-sdk for now. But we can't use openstack-sdk in this spec, because openstack-sdk's method name has no relationship with url.
YAML parser is what I intend to build in this spec. So, yes, validator is port of the YAML parser. I don't get your point here.

A good practice is to list all references explicitly.

Others, please see my later response below.

Do you mean, for example:

http://.../host?vimid=id1&tenantid=id2&host=id3?

(parameters are the variables in URL)

I think we do. From RESTful perspective, all CRUD operations are expected for the resources. If one creates a resource, one should be able to delete, update and get resources. That's the entire lifecycle operations of a resource expected by a tenant.

That's why I commented previously that we shouldn't duplicate those CRUD operation, or HTTP method in this spec. Because we are compliant with RESTful convention, we may only need to add RESTful convention in the reference.

That is my biggest concern.

Here the work item is "Elastic API Exposure", the previous sections "Problem Description" and "Proposed Change" all lead to making a normal reader think it is a general framework for extending APIs.

In the "Definition of API" part, "host" is used as an example.

Perhaps "host" itself may be a normative definition for now. But what is the normative definition of the framework?

For example, when I write a YAML, what is the root object that covers every branch, where "host" belongs to one of the branches?

If we want to extend the "host" definition, how should I specify the backward compatibility?

Let alone there are many other resources than "host", and what is the structure to aggregate them in a normative format in YAML that supports both extensibility and backward compatibility?

See above, especially "host" is one resource. The "full normative definition" is the one overall structure that aggregates all resources in a format that is extensible and backward compatible.

For example, you may consider "host" as a "function" in one of the source code file in programming, and the "full normative definition" is the whole source code package.

Will API handler call all VIM APIs directly? i.e. call Azure API, call AWS APIs, call OpenStack APIs etc. without going through Plugins?

I thought that once API handler identifies which VIM the operation should go, it would call relevant Plugin, give the control to Plugin and let the Plugin to handle it call the specific VIM APIs.
My point here is: if validator is part of YAML parser, shouldn't they be the same/one work item?

Ok, it sounds like a runtime framework which will consume YAML and expose service to consumers. Another question is: will this runtime framework allow to hook extensions written in python (or some other languages) to the logic defined in YAML? On the other hand, does the developers have to compose all logic by the "language"/model specified by this YAML? Thanks
Sorry, I don't get your point "hook extensions", can you give an example. For the other question, yes, developers have to do that.

But the current multi-cloud doesn't support CRUD for all resources. Take the /hosts as example, only get method is support. So we need to explicitly specify the supported HTTP method here. We don't want to change how the api may looks, in this spec.

I guess I understand your point here. What I try to accomplish here is much simpler approach. The restful resources are all "flat" resources. I don't intent to design a sophisticating model definition here. I guess the current resources of multi-cloud are "flat", I don't see much relationship between them.  For the extensibility and backward compatibility concern, developer can define different YAML file for that.

This spec just want to do one thing, that is provide a new way to expose APIs so that we don't need to use static code to maintain them.
If the change is made in framework, then yes, call will be forwarded to plugin. If the change is made to plugin, then the plugin needs to interact with VIM.
Yes, it could be. I thought, maybe I could get all the data first, for example, get the "minimal" data first in YAML parser, and validate the value in this work item.

You answered my question. That is exactly my concern or more precisely, my confusion. Imaging the case that if the API need do more things in addition to translating NBI to SBI, unfortunately it cannot be captured in that YAML, what should we do about that?

I'm not sure why this proposal solves this issue.
Even if yaml format is adapted, the yaml file will be maintained manually?

What's the advantage of this proposal over swagger yaml?
swagger support yaml format instead of json.
Why don't we just adapt swagger yaml?

Can you please elaborate 'dynamically'?
Will REST API controller will be constructed at server startup time instead of pre-compilartion?

Probably you'd like to check openstack gluon.
nice. Now I think the meaning of "dynamic" is clear.

So is the following definition different from swagger?
i.e. https://swagger.io/docs/specification/paths-and-operations/

If same, we can just include pointer.
If no, why? and what's the advantage of this our own definition?

I am trying to follow swagger convention here. Do you mean why we need a swagger yaml here and keep the swagger json file at the same time?

If so, then the answer is MSB project will read MultiCloud's api definition by a constant url, like "api/multicloud/v0/swagger.json". I don't intent to change that for now.

Suggest to add the following paragraph at Line 63 here, based on prior comments and agreement in comments to take reference to prior art in industry:

"
It should be noted that there is a prior art in OpenStack "Gluon" [1]_ project which provides a model-driven framework to generate APIs based on model definitions in YAML. A full, normative definition and extension mechanism of "API Specification" [2]_ is available in Gluon. Although our current work has limited scope, for those who are interested in full normative definition and extension mechanism in our future work, please refer to those references in "Gluon" [1]_ project and its "API Specifications" [2]_.
"
It would also be good to add Testing part, i.e. how to test those newly generated APIs, CSIT etc.

Suggest to add the reference section at the end:

"
References
==========

.. [1] https://wiki.openstack.org/wiki/Gluon
.. [2] https://github.com/openstack/gluon/blob/master/doc/source/devref/gluon_api_spec.inc
"

No. I understand the necessity of it.
My question is, is your schema suggested by below example same to swagger yaml's or not?

From the above your comment, the answer is yes, right?
So please write it down explicitly(e.g. the schema of swagger yaml is adapted and its schema definition can be found at this url)
Otherwise, with only the below example, I couldn't figure out,
you adapted swagger yaml schema definition or you (re-)invented your own schema definition or you started from swagger yaml schema, but twisted it.
What is your intention?


the section of "swagger file generation" implies it. but not explicit.


To implement this proposal, formal schema definition is required to validate if the given yaml file for API definition is valid or not.
Are there any OSS tools available?
Isn't there any OSS tools for this?
Will add, gluon will be a good reference if a full normative definition is required.
The plan is to adapt swagger schema, will update the spec, thanks for pointing out.
plan is to test it with volte case. but I am not sure if it is part of CSIT.
I plan to use pyyaml to parse the yaml file. For the rest client, I plan to reuse the existing code. Any suggestion is welcomed here.
No plan for now, just some plain code to do the conversion.
Done

Please elaborate the term "extension support". With my understanding of "extension support", it would allow the code library to be plugged into yaml to implement the API exposed in this yaml. If my understanding is correct, I didn't find out how this "extension support" will be supported in "Definition of API" .
There
Your understanding is correct. Check the section "vim_path" I will consolidate the description in following update.
Done
Is there always code generation? I mean generating some runtime data can also achieve the elastic api exposure.
There is no post/put/delete for host in Multicloud for now, does this mean  the model definition will extend the capability of Multicloud.
not sure if these examples are appropriate.  But there is no such interface resource in Multi-cloud for now.
